title: Salary & Compensation trends
text:
This section focuses on the following questions:

How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?
What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?
Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?
What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?

How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers


ai_keywords = [
    "ai", "artificial intelligence", "machine learning", "ml",
    "deep learning", "nlp", "computer vision", "generative ai",
    "data scientist", "machine learning engineer", "ai engineer"
]

df["TITLE_CLEAN"] = df["TITLE_CLEAN"].astype(str)

df["ai_flag"] = df["TITLE_CLEAN"].str.lower().apply(
    lambda x: "AI-related" if any(k in x for k in ai_keywords) else "Non-AI"
)

salary_ai = (
    df.groupby("ai_flag")["salary_annual"]
      .mean()
      .reset_index()
      .sort_values("salary_annual", ascending=False)
)

fig = px.bar(
    salary_ai,
    x="ai_flag",
    y="salary_annual",
    title="Average Annual Salary: AI-related vs. Non-AI Careers",
    labels={"ai_flag": "Career Type", "salary_annual": "Average Annual Salary"}
)
fig.show()

                            
                                            



df["salary_annual"].max()

np.float64(718800.0)

title: Data Cleaning
text:
This section describes the data cleaning process for the Lightcast job postings dataset.
The goal is to keep the most relevant variables, remove duplicate records, standardize missing values, and create a consistent annual salary variable for later analysis.

title: Data Cleaning
text:
This section describes the data cleaning process for the Lightcast job postings dataset.
The goal is to keep the most relevant variables, remove duplicate records, standardize missing values, and create a consistent annual salary variable for later analysis.

title: Data Cleaning
text:
Import libraries
We first import the Python libraries needed for data processing. Then we load the raw Lightcast job postings dataset.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_csv("data/raw/lightcast_job_postings.csv", low_memory=False)
print("Original shape:", df.shape)

Original shape: (72498, 131)



keep_cols = [
    "ID",
    "POSTED",
    "EXPIRED",
    "DURATION",
    "TITLE_RAW",
    "TITLE_CLEAN",
    "COMPANY_NAME",
    "SALARY",
    "SALARY_FROM",
    "SALARY_TO",
    "ORIGINAL_PAY_PERIOD",
    "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",
    "LOCATION",
    "CITY_NAME",
    "STATE_NAME",
    "NAICS2_NAME",
    "NAICS3_NAME",
    "LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME",
    "SKILLS_NAME",
    "SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME",
    "COMMON_SKILLS_NAME",
    "SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME",
    "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME"
]

df = df[keep_cols].copy()
print("Shape after selecting columns:", df.shape)

Shape after selecting columns: (72498, 25)


Some job postings may appear more than once in the dataset. To avoid double-counting, I remove duplicate rows based on the unique job ID. Also, some missing values appear as blank strings instead of standard missing values. This step converts blank entries to NaN so they can be handled consistently.

df = df.drop_duplicates(subset="ID")
print("Shape after removing duplicates:", df.shape)

df = df.replace(r"^\s*$", np.nan, regex=True)

Shape after removing duplicates: (72477, 25)


The salary information is not always stored in the same way. Some postings include a single salary value, while others include only a salary range.
The function below creates salary_clean using the following logic:

def get_salary(row):
    if pd.notna(row["SALARY"]):
        return row["SALARY"]
    elif pd.notna(row["SALARY_FROM"]) and pd.notna(row["SALARY_TO"]):
        return (row["SALARY_FROM"] + row["SALARY_TO"]) / 2
    elif pd.notna(row["SALARY_FROM"]):
        return row["SALARY_FROM"]
    elif pd.notna(row["SALARY_TO"]):
        return row["SALARY_TO"]
    else:
        return np.nan

df["salary_clean"] = df.apply(get_salary, axis=1)

The cleaned salary values still use different pay periods such as year, month, week, day, and hour. To make the salary values comparable, I convert all of them to annual salary.

def smart_convert(row):
    salary = row["salary_clean"]
    period = str(row["ORIGINAL_PAY_PERIOD"]).lower()

    if pd.isna(salary):
        return np.nan

    if period == "year":
        return salary

    elif period == "hour" and salary < 500:
        return salary * 2080

    elif period == "day" and salary < 4000:
        return salary * 260

    elif period == "week" and salary < 20000:
        return salary * 52

    elif period == "month" and salary < 60000:
        return salary * 12

    else:
        return salary

df["salary_annual"] = df.apply(smart_convert, axis=1)
df = df[df["salary_annual"].between(20000, 720000)]

Because the pay period field is inconsistent, I use a rule-based conversion approach. Only values that fall within a plausible range for hourly, weekly, or monthly pay are converted to annual salary. Larger values are treated as already annualized.

df["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"] = df["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"].replace("[None]", np.nan)


import ast
import re

def clean_list_column(x):
    if pd.isna(x):
        return np.nan

    try:
        parsed = ast.literal_eval(x)
        if isinstance(parsed, list):
            items = parsed
        else:
            items = [parsed]
    except:
        items = [x]

    cleaned = []
    for s in items:
        if pd.isna(s):
            continue
        s = str(s).strip()
        s = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s).strip()
        if s and s.lower() != "none":
            cleaned.append(s)

    cleaned = list(dict.fromkeys(cleaned))
    return ", ".join(cleaned) if cleaned else np.nan

list_like_cols = [
    "SKILLS_NAME",
    "SPECIALIZED_SKILLS_NAME",
    "COMMON_SKILLS_NAME",
    "SOFTWARE_SKILLS_NAME",
    "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME",
    "LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME"
]

for col in list_like_cols:
    df[col] = df[col].apply(clean_list_column)

def fix_encoding(x):
    if pd.isna(x):
        return x
    try:
        return x.encode("latin1").decode("utf-8")
    except:
        return x


text_cols = ["COMPANY_NAME", "TITLE_RAW", "TITLE_CLEAN", "CITY_NAME", "STATE_NAME"]

for col in text_cols:
    df[col] = df[col].apply(fix_encoding)

The skills variable is stored as a string representation of a list.
To make it usable for analysis, I convert it into a proper list format and then expand it so that each row contains one skill.
Since salary analysis requires a comparable salary measure, I remove rows that still do not have a valid annual salary after the conversion step.

from pathlib import Path

df = df[df["salary_annual"].notna()]
print("Final shape:", df.shape)

Path("data/processed").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

df.to_csv("data/processed/lightcast_cleaned.csv", index=False)
print("Cleaned dataset saved successfully.")

Final shape: (32371, 27)
Cleaned dataset saved successfully.


The cleaned dataset is now ready for later analysis.
